era_context: Early Bronze Age. Egypt's First Dynasty is consolidating pharaonic authority and developing administrative systems. Mesopotamian city-states are competing for regional dominance; Uruk remains the largest urban centre. Cuneiform writing is becoming standardised. The Indus Valley Civilisation is developing urban centres. Most of the world remains preliterate and is known only through archaeology.

events.title: First Dynasty Egypt: consolidation of pharaonic authority and divine kingship
events.description: The First Dynasty pharaohs are consolidating the concept of divine kingship, portraying themselves as intermediaries between the gods and the people. Religious inscriptions and tomb architecture reflect the development of a state religion centred on the pharaoh's divine status. The pharaoh is increasingly depicted in royal regalia and performing religious rituals. This ideological framework will persist throughout Egyptian history.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from temples and tombs

events.certainty_note: The development of divine kingship ideology is well-attested archaeologically, but the precise dating of specific developments is uncertain. This year is assigned by convention to the early-to-middle First Dynasty.

events.title: Mesopotamian city-state competition and territorial disputes
events.description: Mesopotamian city-states continue to compete for control of agricultural land and trade routes. Early Dynastic II period (c. 3200–2900 BCE) is marked by increasing military conflict and fortification of cities. Cuneiform records document disputes over water rights and territorial boundaries. These conflicts establish patterns of inter-city competition that will persist for centuries.

sources.name: Cuneiform tablets documenting disputes and campaigns
Archaeological evidence of fortifications and destruction layers

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of city-state competition is well-attested, but specific campaigns and their dates are difficult to assign to individual years. Dating is approximate based on stratigraphic and textual evidence.

disconfirming_evidence: The extent and intensity of Early Dynastic military conflict in Mesopotamia is debated. Some scholars argue that the 'conflict' narrative is overstated and that cooperation and trade were equally important. The concept of divine kingship in Egypt may have developed more gradually than the archaeological record suggests; it is not clear that all First Dynasty pharaohs were portrayed as divine.
historiographic_note: Documentation remains extremely sparse. The historical record is based on archaeological evidence (tombs, fortifications, pottery, architectural remains) and early administrative records (cuneiform tablets, Egyptian inscriptions). These sources are biased toward elite activities, monumental construction, and religious ideology. The vast majority of human activity—agriculture, craft production, daily life—is invisible in the written record and must be inferred from archaeology. Regional coverage is heavily skewed toward Egypt and Mesopotamia; other regions are known only through archaeology.
